ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chives broadly; no dedicated Chinese chives / Allium tuberosum code exists at species level. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of Chinese chives is approximately 85 g, equivalent to a small bowl of stir-fried or blanched chives as served in a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Garlic chives, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 11241)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for garlic chives (Allium tuberosum), raw, per 100 g. Values represent the raw vegetable; cooked preparations will vary. This is the closest approved primary source for Chinese chives as a raw ingredient. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a standalone Chinese chives entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Vegetables — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for leafy green vegetables and allium family vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI test for Chinese chives (Allium tuberosum) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 15 based on the established pattern for non-starchy allium vegetables (onion GI ~10, leek GI ~15, chives analogous) and the very low available carbohydrate content of 4.15 g per 100 g. Glycemic load per 85 g serving = (15 × 4.15 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 0.5. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for all non-starchy leafy green vegetables in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g) and high potassium (477 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Chinese chives are an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension due to their negligible sodium content and high potassium level, which supports healthy blood pressure regulation; however, restaurant preparations may add salt or sauces that significantly increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (estimated 15) and low available carbohydrate (4.15 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chinese chives have a very low glycemic index and minimal glycemic load per serving, making them a safe and beneficial vegetable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; their dietary fibre content (2.4 g per 100 g) further supports postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.09 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, high vitamin C and folate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chinese chives are cardioprotective due to their negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with meaningful levels of vitamin C (58.1 mg), folate (95 mcg DFE), and potassium, which collectively support endothelial function and reduce cardiovascular risk; restaurant stir-fried preparations with oil or oyster sauce should be consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese chives (Allium tuberosum), known in Mandarin as 韭菜 (jiǔcài), are a leafy green vegetable widely used in Chinese, Southeast Asian, and East Asian cuisines. Unlike Western chives (Allium schoenoprasum), Chinese chives have flat, broad leaves with a distinctive garlic-like aroma and flavour, which is why they are also commonly called garlic chives. The plant produces edible leaves, flower buds, and stems, all of which are used in cooking. In Chinese cuisine, Chinese chives are stir-fried with eggs, pork, tofu, or seafood; used as a filling in dumplings (jiaozi) and pancakes; blanched and served as a side dish; or used as a garnish. In Singapore, they appear in dishes such as char kway teow and oyster omelette. Nutritionally, Chinese chives are low in calories (30 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (4.15 g per 100 g), making them suitable for low-glycaemic diets. They are a good source of vitamin C (58.1 mg per 100 g), vitamin A (218 mcg RAE per 100 g), folate (95 mcg DFE per 100 g), potassium (477 mg per 100 g), and dietary fibre (2.4 g per 100 g). Their very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g) makes them appropriate for hypertension management. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), consistent with other non-starchy allium vegetables. Clinically, Chinese chives are beneficial for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease when prepared without excessive oil or high-sodium sauces.

Are Chinese chives good for diabetics?
How many calories in Chinese chives?
What is the GI of Chinese chives?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy for garlic chives (Allium tuberosum) raw, which is an exact species match. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database patterns for non-starchy allium vegetables, as no direct GI test for Chinese chives exists in the approved database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the GI estimation and the fact that the field survey listing context (Riverfront Seafood, Boat Quay) suggests a restaurant-prepared dish that may differ nutritionally from raw values.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent raw Chinese chives per USDA SR Legacy; cooked restaurant preparations (stir-fried with oil, oyster sauce, or garlic) will have higher energy, fat, and sodium values. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for this raw vegetable. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant food with no B12. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; Chinese chives are not a meaningful source of vitamin D. 6. sugar_g is 0.0 — USDA SR Legacy reports 0 g sugar for garlic chives raw; confirmed zero per source. 7. GI is estimated at 15 based on analogous non-starchy allium vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI test exists for Chinese chives. 8. The field survey listing at Riverfront Seafood (Boat Quay) at SGD 17.60 suggests this is a restaurant-prepared dish, likely stir-fried with seafood or as a side; the nutrient record reflects the raw vegetable baseline. 9. Monounsaturated fat (0.02 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.37 g) are reported per USDA source. 10. Korean local name is not populated as Chinese chives are not a distinctively Korean-named food in this context; the food is primarily Chinese in origin.
